eli3: Fraud detection systems identify and block fraudulent transactions in real-time. Credit cards, insurance claims, account takeovers — catching fraud fast saves billions and protects customers.

Unique challenges: extreme class imbalance (0.1% fraud vs 99.9% legitimate), adversarial attackers constantly adapting, and high cost of false positives (declining valid transactions angers customers).
Architecture: real-time scoring (<50ms) for transaction approval, with feedback loop from investigator decisions improving models. Must balance fraud loss vs customer friction.

whenWhy.title: Payment fraud
whenWhy.body: Credit/debit card transactions: detect stolen cards, card-not-present fraud, merchant fraud. Real-time authorization (approve/decline in <100ms). 3D Secure step-up authentication for suspicious transactions. Global card fraud losses: ~$30B annually.

whenWhy.title: Insurance fraud
whenWhy.body: Claims fraud: staged accidents, inflated damages, fake injuries. Predict at claim submission, investigate high-risk. Save insurers billions in payouts. Identify fraud rings via network analysis.

whenWhy.title: Identity and account takeover
whenWhy.body: Detect suspicious login attempts, account creation fraud, synthetic identities. Behavioral biometrics (typing patterns, mouse movements) for continuous authentication. Prevent account takeover before damage done.

whenWhy.title: Anti-money laundering (AML)
whenWhy.body: Detect structuring (smurfing), layering, integration in money laundering. Monitor transactions over time (not just single event). Suspicious Activity Reports (SARs) to regulators. Compliance requirement for banks.

deepDiveIntro: Fraud detection ML: (1) Features: transaction amount, merchant category, location, time, velocity (transactions per hour), device fingerprint, behavioral biometrics. (2) Models: gradient boosting (XGBoost, LightGBM) popular for tabular data, neural networks for sequences, graph neural networks for networks. (3) Real-time: feature store for low-latency lookups, model serving with <50ms latency. (4) Review queues: medium-risk transactions to human investigators.

Adversarial aspect: fraudsters adapt to detection. Model performance degrades as attackers learn. Solutions: continuous retraining, online learning, model ensembles, feature obfuscation (don't leak detection logic).

deepDive.heading: Handling Class Imbalance
deepDive.body: Fraud rate 0.01-1%. Standard accuracy misleading (99% accuracy by predicting all legitimate). Solutions: (1) Sampling: SMOTE, undersampling majority. (2) Class weights: higher weight for fraud class. (3) Threshold tuning: optimize for F1, precision-recall, not accuracy. (4) Cost-sensitive learning: weight by business cost (false positive vs false negative costs).

deepDive.heading: Feature Engineering
deepDive.body: Velocity: transaction count/amount in last hour/day (rapid fire = suspicious). Sequence: time since last transaction, location distance (impossible travel). Device: fingerprint, new device flag. Behavioral: typing speed, swipe patterns (biometrics). Network: connected users, shared attributes (device, address). Aggregations: merchant category spending patterns.

deepDive.heading: Model Architecture
deepDive.body: GBDT (XGBoost/LightGBM): strong baseline for tabular fraud features. Handles missing values, non-linear interactions. Neural networks: autoencoders for anomaly detection (reconstruction error), RNNs for transaction sequences. Graph: GNNs for fraud rings (connected fraudulent accounts). Ensemble: combine models for robustness.

deepDive.heading: Real-Time Scoring
deepDive.body: Latency budget: <50ms for authorization decision. Feature store: pre-computed aggregations, sub-10ms lookup. Model serving: lightweight models (LightGBM) or distilled neural nets. Rules engine: hard-coded business rules first (fast path), ML for complex cases. Fallback: if model fails, default to rules (don't block payments).

deepDive.heading: Adversarial Adaptation
deepDive.body: Fraudsters test system, learn thresholds, adapt behavior. Countermeasures: (1) Model rotation: deploy new models frequently, don't reveal which is active. (2) Online learning: adapt immediately to new patterns. (3) Feature randomization: different features per transaction. (4) Honeypots: fake data to catch attackers. (5) Continuous monitoring: track fraud rate, model drift, adversarial patterns.

takeaways: Extreme class imbalance: use sampling, class weights, cost-sensitive learning.
Real-time: <50ms scoring with feature stores and lightweight models.
Features: velocity, sequences, device fingerprints, behavioral biometrics.
Adversarial: fraudsters adapt, require continuous retraining and online learning.
Balance: fraud loss vs false positive customer friction.

edgeCases.q: What about false positives blocking legitimate customers?
edgeCases.a: High business cost: angry customers, abandoned transactions, lost revenue. Mitigation: (1) Confidence thresholds: only auto-decline high-confidence fraud. (2) Step-up authentication: 3D Secure, SMS code for medium risk (friction but not decline). (3) Graduated response: soft decline (ask to retry) vs hard decline. (4) Customer communication: explain why, easy appeal process. (5) Monitor false positive rate as key metric.

edgeCases.q: How do you handle concept drift in fraud?
edgeCases.a: Severe: fraudsters constantly innovate. Solutions: (1) Online learning: adapt model daily/hourly. (2) Ensemble: combine recent and old models. (3) Feature drift monitoring: alert when patterns change. (4) Champion/challenger: test new models continuously. (5) Feedback loop: investigator labels improve model quickly. (6) Behavioral features (harder to spoof) more stable than static features.

Individual transaction may look legitimate, but network reveals fraud. Solutions: (1) Graph analysis: shared devices, addresses, IP subnets. (2) GNNs: learn embeddings based on connections. (3) Community detection: identify clusters of related accounts. (4) Link analysis: visualize connections for investigators. (5) Collective inference: classify accounts jointly, not independently.

sources.label: Phua et al. — A Comprehensive Survey of Data Mining-based Fraud Detection Research
Bolton & Hand — Statistical Fraud Detection: A Review
